content.problemStatement:
The String street is known as the busiest street in Codeland.
Tourists from all over the world want to visit the street once they are in Codeland.
The Chef owns **N** souvenir stores across the street (numbered from **1** to **N**). 
At the beginning there is no souvenir in any store, the Chef has some plans to add some new items.
Each the Chef's plan is represented by 4 numbers: **u v a b** which mean an items with price **b** 
is added to the store **u**, an items with price **a + b** is added to the store **u + 1** and so on. 
More formally, an item with price **a * i + b** is added to the store **u + i** for all (**0 ≤ i ≤ v - u**).

In additional to the cost of the item itself, the tourist must pay some conservation fees as well.
The Codeland regularly defines the new conservation fee. Each fee is represented by 4 numbers: **u v a b** which means
the tourist buying any item in the store **u + i** will be charged a fee of **i * a + b** for all (**0 ≤ i ≤ v - u**).
In the case that several conservation fees have effect on the same store, the customer needs to pay all of those fees.

At some point of time, a tourist at store **i** asks you what is the **largest** amount of money they have to spend for 
a souvenir at that store (the amount of money includes the price of one of the souvenirs and all the conservation fees for that store).
constraints: For events of type 1: 1 ≤ u ≤ v ≤ N, |a|, |b| ≤ 10^9
For events of type 2: 1 ≤ u ≤ v ≤ N, |a|, |b| ≤ 10^4
For events of type 3: 1 ≤ i ≤ N
content.inputFormat: First line contains two integers N and M, representing the number of stores and the number of events. Each of the next M lines represents an event of three types: the new plan of the Chef, the new conservation fee, or the query from tourist.
content.outputFormat: For each query from tourist, print in one line the corresponding answer. If there is no item at the ith store, print out 'NA' as the answer.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The first query is 'NA' because there are no items in store 5. The second query is 7 because the item with price 7 is added to store 5. The third query is still 7 because the item with price 2 is added to store 5, but it is cheaper than the item with price 7. The fourth query is -38 because the item with price -38 is added to store 10. The fifth query is 11 because the conservation fee of 1 is added to store 6. The sixth query is 14 because the conservation fee of 3 is added to store 6.

The first query is 7 because the item with price 7 is added to store 3. The second query is 12 because the item with price 12 is added to store 3. The third query is 7 because the item with price 7 is still the most expensive item in store 3. The fourth query is 13 because the conservation fee of 1 is added to store 3.

The first query is 'NA' because there are no items in store 5. The second query is 7 because the item with price 7 is added to store 5. The third query is still 7 because the item with price 2 is added to store 5, but it is cheaper than the item with price 7. The fourth query is -38 because the item with price -38 is added to store 10. The fifth query is 11 because the conservation fee of 1 is added to store 6. The sixth query is 14 because the conservation fee of 3 is added to store 6.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved by maintaining a list of items and their corresponding prices for each store. When a new plan is added, we update the prices of the items in the affected stores. When a new conservation fee is added, we update the conservation fee for the affected stores. When a query is made, we find the maximum price of an item in the store plus the conservation fee.
solution.timeComplexity: O(M * N) per test case, O(M * N) total. No precomputation is done.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — the dominant structure is the list of stores.
